paper_title: SurGe: Improved Surface Geometry in Point Maps

sections.title: Abstract
sections.content: Recent feedforward 3D reconstruction methods predict point maps and estimate global 3D geometry remarkably well. However, their predictions still exhibit inaccurate local surface geometry, which is clearly visible qualitatively but only weakly reflected in common metrics. To make these errors more explicit in evaluation, we introduce a point map normal metric that evaluates the local surface orientation induced by neighboring 3D predictions. To reduce these errors, we propose two complementary components: a point gradient matching loss that supervises depth-normalized 3D finite differences, and a Neighborhood Attention Decoder (NAD) that progressively upsamples features and uses Neighborhood Attention for local feature mixing. Across eight zero-shot monocular geometry benchmarks, our model, SurGe, achieves the best average rank for global point map AbsRel and consistently improves local point map and point map normal evaluations.

sections.title: Start
sections.content: SurGe: Improved Surface Geometry in Point Maps Karim Knaebel1 Gonzalo Martin Garcia1 Christian Schmidt1 Ilya Fradlin1 Lucas Nunes Daan de Geus2 Bastian Leibe1 1RWTH Aachen University 2Eindhoven University of Technology https://vision.rwth-aachen.de/surge

sections.title: Abstract
sections.content: Recent feedforward 3D reconstruction methods predict point maps and estimate global 3D geometry remarkably well. However, their predictions still exhibit inaccurate local surface geometry, which is clearly visible qualitatively but only weakly reflected in common metrics. To make these errors more explicit in evaluation, we introduce point map normal metric that evaluates the local surface orientation induced by neighboring 3D predictions. To reduce these errors, we propose two complementary components: point gradient matching loss that supervises depth-normalized 3D finite differences, and Neighborhood Attention Decoder (NAD) that progressively upsamples features and uses Neighborhood Attention for local feature mixing. Across eight zero-shot monocular geometry benchmarks, our model, SurGe, achieves the best average rank for global point map AbsRel and consistently improves local point map and point map normal evaluations. 6 2 0 2 9 2 ] . [ 1 7 7 5 1 3 . 5 0 6 2 : r (a) SurGe (b) MoGe-2 [51] (c) InfiniDepth [63] Figure 1: Qualitative state-of-the-art comparison. SurGe predicts noticeably cleaner point maps. Preprint.

sections.title: Introduction
sections.content: Monocular geometry estimation seeks to recover dense 3D scene structure from single image. Recent feedforward models [31, 50, 51] predict point map, assigning each pixel 3D point. This representation was initially proposed for two-view geometry by DUSt3R [52]; it was then brought to monocular geometry by MoGe [50], and was extended to the many-view setting by VGGT [47]. Since then, the area has developed rapidly, with growing number of follow-up models building on the same general paradigm [20, 24, 54]. While these models perform strongly overall and generalize well to in-the-wild images, their predictions still contain visible local 3D artifacts despite having plausible coarse scene geometry  (Fig. 1)  . In particular, small deviations in the relative positions of nearby predicted points can distort local shape and orientation. We refer to the relative 3D structure of nearby points as local surface geometry and focus on improving it in this work. This weakness is especially visible on thin structures. Thin foreground elements such as streetlamps, street signs, chair legs, or faucets often emerge bent or oscillatory in 3D, and the distortion typically becomes more severe as the structure gets thinner and the background is farther away. We find that this is not 2D edge-localization problem but 3D shape problem: the point prediction can look plausible when viewed as depth map while neighboring 3D points form inconsistent surface patches. We hypothesize that errors in local surface geometry have received limited attention because standard point map metrics evaluate local surface geometry only indirectly. They measure whether predicted points have accurate 3D positions on average after global alignment. However, neighboring points can fail to form coherent surface even when the individual point errors are small on average. Surface ripples, blockiness, and distortions can arise from relatively small point displacements, so their contribution to an average point-position error can be weak compared to ordinary placement errors. The local point map metric of Wang et al. [50] makes this evaluation more fine-grained by aligning segmented instances separately, but after alignment it still averages pointwise residuals. We therefore complement pointwise metrics with point map normal metric computed from neighboring point differences, which directly evaluates the local surface orientation induced by the predicted point map. In normal-based metric, artifacts are measured through the changes they induce in local surface orientation, rather than through the magnitude of the underlying point-position residuals; Fig. 2 illustrates this with lowand high-frequency perturbations of the ground-truth surface. Equipped with metric that better tracks local structure, we revisit the loss formulation of recent models. Like the global point map metrics, commonly used global point losses [50, 52] measure average point positioning error and are dominated by global geometry. As result, they require additional losses to enforce local consistency and to suppress oscillations and surface irregularities. Common surface losses include terms on normals estimated from point maps and edge angles [50, 51] as well as several gradient matching variants [23, 34]. However, none of these existing losses provides sufficiently strong surface supervision for point maps. Interestingly, preliminary experiments showed that the log-depth gradient matching loss originally proposed for monocular depth [23] does improve local surface geometry reliably, but tends to harm global geometry as it is not compatible with the point map formulation. In this work, we adapt this loss to enable its direct application to point maps, while preserving its pairwise scale invariance. We find that this improved supervision helps, but does not remove the main failure mode. Therefore, we also investigate the architectural design of the decoder. Recent point map models typically pair ViT backbone with convolutional decoder such as MoGes ConvStack [50] or DPT head [33]. We find that even with improved surface losses, these decoders still struggle to recover the 3D shape of thin structures in front of distant or complex backgrounds. We hypothesize that convolution-based decoders struggle here because these regions require reconstructing high-frequency, high-amplitude signal in image space, which is difficult to represent in fixed convolutional kernels. Scaling the convolutional decoder helps, but quickly reaches diminishing returns. ViT decoder such as in π3 [54] avoids fixed filters, but it processes all features at single low-resolution scale determined by the patch size, which leads to patch-aligned artifacts. Our solution is to keep the progressive multiscale decoding structure of convolution-based decoders, while replacing convolutions with blocks based on Neighborhood Attention [15]. This yields decoder that can selectively aggregate local evidence unlike convolutional decoders, without incurring the cost of full-resolution self-attention and without producing patch-aligned artifacts as pure ViT decoder. In practice, it produces more faithful thin structures and more stable local surface geometry than 2 Ground Truth Low-Frequency Perturbation High-Frequency Perturbation AbsRelglob 0.62% AbsRelloc 2.37% MAEnormal 19.0 AbsRelglob 0.60% AbsRelloc 2.39% MAEnormal 85.1 M o a N t P Figure 2: Pointwise metrics only weakly capture local surface geometry. We add lowand high-frequency perturbations to the same ground-truth point map. AbsRelglob and AbsRelloc average pointwise position errors, giving nearly identical scores even though the high-frequency perturbation yields much less coherent local surface geometry. MAEnormal instead compares point map normals induced by neighboring point differences, and therefore reflects this degradation. convolutional decoders. Combined with our point gradient matching loss, the resulting model sets new state of the art in the local point map and point map normal evaluations, while also achieving the best results in the global point map evaluation on several common zero-shot benchmarks. Our contributions are as follows: 1. An evaluation metric based on point map normals that better reflects local surface quality than standard point map metrics (MAEnormal, Eq. (4)). 2. scale-invariant point gradient matching loss, inspired by log-depth gradient matching [23], for supervising local surface structure in point maps (Lpgm, Eq. (3)). 3. decoder based on Neighborhood Attention for dense point map prediction that improves thin structures and local surface geometry (NAD, Sec. 3.1).

sections.title: 2 Related Work
sections.content: 2.1 Feedforward Geometry Estimation Geometry estimation is foundational task for many applications, including robotics, autonomous driving, and virtual reality. Many state-of-the-art architectures combine plain ViT initialized from DINOv2 [29] with convolutional decoder. This decoder is often implemented as DPT head [3, 33, 60, 61], ConvStack [50, 51], or similar convolutional multi-resolution architecture [31]. Notable exceptions to this are InfiniDepth [63], which uses query-based decoder that decodes depth for individual continuous image coordinates, and Pixel-Perfect Depth (PPD) [58], which uses decoder inspired by the DiT architecture [30]. InfiniDepth and PPD predict affine-invariant depth in log space instead of point maps. Unprojecting affine-invariant log-depth to 3D geometry requires reference point map; both models use MoGe-2 to estimate this reference point map [58, 63]. While InfiniDepth and PPD aim to improve fine-grained geometry estimation, we observe blocky surface artifacts for InfiniDepth and severe surface noise for PPD. We show quantitative comparison in Sec. 4 and qualitative examples in Sec. F. Current multi-view methods also achieve impressive results in the monocular setting. Their architecture follows similar scheme as monocular models, combining ViT encoder with an inter-frame feature fusion module and per-frame decoder. DUSt3R [52] uses ViT pretrained with cross-view completion [55, 56] and DPT head for point map predictions. It predicts point maps from two 3 input views in shared coordinate system. Follow-up works employ global attention over all frames, enabling faster processing of large image sets [20, 24, 47, 54, 59]. Similar to the previous two-view and monocular methods, these methods often use DPT head to regress one point map per input image [20, 24, 47, 59]. notable exception is π3 [54], which combines plain Transformer [46] with final pixel shuffle [38] for point predictions. The impressive performance of these methods illustrates the tremendous progress in geometry estimation thanks to strong backbones, improved feature fusion modules, large-scale training, and improved supervision. However, we still observe poor predictions for local surface geometry, especially around thin structures (see Fig. 1). Compared to previous methods, our Neighborhood Attention Decoder (NAD) visibly improves in this regard. Unlike DPT heads and ConvStack decoders, it is not restricted to fixed convolutional kernels, and unlike ViT decoders, it is not plagued by patching artifacts. 2.2 Evaluation of Local Surface Geometry Point map metrics used in recent work remain pointwise after alignment: global affine-invariant AbsRel evaluates all valid pixels, while the local point map metric of Wang et al. [50] evaluates instance-mask regions after separate local alignment. This improves sensitivity to errors inside individual object masks, but still does not measure whether neighboring predictions form coherent surface. Figure 2 illustrates this limitation and motivates the point map normal metric in Eq. (4). 2.3 Point Map Supervision DUSt3R [52] popularized the point map representation for 3D geometry estimation and introduced the corresponding point map loss as direct regression of aligned or normalized 3D points. Many follow-up works adapt this point map loss with different output parameterizations [20, 24, 47]. The residuals, however, remain pointwise and do not directly constrain whether neighboring predictions form coherent local surface. Prior methods therefore add surface losses: point normals and edge angle losses supervise local orientation but do not constrain displacement magnitude [24, 50, 51]; other depth and point map methods compare finite differences after one shared alignment or normalization of the whole prediction [34, 47], but this does not reward local coherence in globally misplaced regions. Log-depth gradient matching compares finite differences of log depth, yielding pairwise scale-invariant residual [23]. In Sec. 3.2, we adapt this pairwise idea to vector-valued point maps. 2.4 Neighborhood Attention In Neighborhood Attention (NA) [15], each query only attends to its local neighborhood. It can be viewed as way to introduce inductive bias into attention or to speed up computations due to sparsity [1417]. To introduce inductive bias, NA can be used to implement hierarchical feature encoders [14, 15]. As sparse attention variant, it has been used to replace full attention in isometric Transformer architectures such as video diffusion models [17]. Dilated NA has been used with encoder-decoder architectures as an efficient drop-in for regular attention, for example, in medical imaging [8, 36] and image restoration [25]. NAF [5] proposes decoder that utilizes NA to cross-attend between high-resolution image features of small CNN and low-resolution feature map of vision foundation model in order to compute high-resolution features. Compared to these works, our Neighborhood Attention Decoder (NAD, Sec. 3.1) neither cross-attends between resolutions nor uses dilated NA; instead, it uses plain NA to make attentionbased mixing practical in multi-stage dense geometry decoder, where high-resolution stages make global self-attention prohibitive. To our knowledge, we are the first to demonstrate the effectiveness of NA as the core mixing mechanism in this setting.

sections.title: 3 Method
sections.content: Our model, SurGe, predicts dense point map ˆP RHW 3, mapping every pixel of single image RHW 3 to its corresponding 3D point. It combines DINOv2 [29]-initialized ViT [9] encoder with Neighborhood Attention Decoder (NAD, Sec. 3.1). We train it with global and local point map losses and our proposed point gradient matching loss (Lpgm, Eq. (3)). 4 Figure 3: SurGe architecture overview. SurGe combines DINOv2 [29] encoder with our Neighborhood Attention Decoder (NAD). NAD upsamples encoder features through sequence of stages ℓ {1, . . . , 5}, each built from nℓ NAD blocks. Compared to standard ViT [9] blocks, NAD blocks replace global self-attention with Neighborhood Attention [15], use window-matched RoPE [40] and only QK normalization [6] instead of pre-attention and pre-feedforward LayerNorm [1]. 3.1 Architecture 16 Figure 3 visualizes the architecture of SurGe. Following recent feedforward 3D reconstruction methods [47, 50], we use ViT-Large backbone [9] initialized from DINOv2 [29]. Given I, the encoder produces low-resolution token grid 16 C. The decoder must then process and upsample this grid to produce per-pixel point predictions ˆP. Recent dense prediction decoders do so with progressive multi-resolution design [31, 33, 50], which we keep. The remaining design choice is how to mix features inside each stage of this decoder. Existing methods struggle with images containing thin structures, where foreground and background evidence are placed in the same small image neighborhood. We hypothesize that feature-dependent attention can separate this evidence more easily than spatially shared convolutional kernels. Therefore, SurGe adopts attention in its decoder. In practice, we use Neighborhood Attention rather than self-attention to obtain contentdependent local mixing at linear cost. Notably, as this progressive decoder processes features at multiple resolutions, it prevents patch-aligned artifacts, unlike single-resolution ViT decoders. We refer to the resulting decoder as Neighborhood Attention Decoder (NAD). Starting from the encoder token grid Z, NAD consists of five stages that produce the predicted point map ˆP, with stages 1 through 4 upsampling by factor of 2. Following Wang et al. [50], the final output projection predicts (ξ, η, ρ) and maps it to points as (ξeρ, ηeρ, eρ). Each stage ℓ operates on feature map Xℓ 25ℓ Cℓ and contains nℓ NAD blocks. For SurGe, C1, . . . , C5 = (1024, 512, 256, 128, 64) and n1 = = n5 = 3. For ℓ < 5, the NAD blocks are followed by an upsampling module that doubles the spatial resolution and halves the channel dimension. We implement this module as transposed 2 2 convolution with stride 2, followed by 3 3 convolution [28]. 25ℓ Each NAD block is Transformer-style [46] residual block with Neighborhood Attention layer and pointwise FFN. It uses window-matched RoPE [40] on queries and keys, and omits the usual pre-attention and pre-FFN LayerNorm [1] layers. Across all stages, the attention window is = 9 and the head dimension is dh = 64; at stage ℓ, the FFN hidden dimension is 4Cℓ. Removing LayerNorm empirically improves accuracy; we hypothesize that dense point regression benefits when activations can retain magnitude cues related to scene geometry. To stabilize attention without pre-attention normalization, we use QK normalization [6, 65]. Additional details on stage-wise positional embeddings, the RoPE temperature, and normalization choices are given in Sec. B. 5 3.2 Loss Following Wang et al. [50], we use the global affine-invariant point map loss Lglob with ROE [50] alignment, as well as local patch losses Lloc,4, Lloc,16, and Lloc,64 with diameters 1 64 of the image diagonal. These losses supervise point positions under global and local affine-invariant alignments, but their residuals are still pointwise after alignment. They therefore only weakly constrain whether neighboring pixels form locally consistent 3D surface. We address this by adding surface loss on neighboring 3D point differences. 16 , and 1 4 , 1 Surface supervision. Existing surface losses make different trade-offs. Losses on normals estimated from point maps and on edge angles [50, 51] compare local surface orientation, but discard the magnitude of the underlying 3D displacements. Some prior depth and point map methods compare finite differences after one shared alignment or normalization of the whole prediction [24, 34, 47]. This asks each local pair to match its target finite difference under the same aligned frame, even when that neighborhood is locally coherent but misplaced relative to the whole scene. For local surface supervision, we instead want the comparison to be defined by the neighboring pair itself. Li and Snavely [23] define such pairwise comparison for ground-truth depth map and predicted depth map ˆD through the log-depth gradient matching loss: (cid:12) 1 (cid:12)x log ˆDij log Dij (cid:12) 2Vy (cid:12) (cid:12)y log ˆDij log Dij (cid:12) Lgm( ˆD, D) = 1 2Vx (cid:12) (cid:12) (cid:12), (1) (cid:12) (cid:12) (cid:12) + (cid:88) (cid:88) (i,j)Vx (i,j)Vy where x, are forward finite differences, and Vx, Vy contain annotated pixels whose horizontal or vertical forward neighbor is also annotated. The logarithm makes each residual depend on neighboring pairs depth ratio rather than global prediction scale, but this construction is tied to positive scalar depth. Point maps are vector-valued and coordinate-wise logarithms or ratios are not geometrically meaningful. Restricting the loss to the coordinate, as in MapAnything [20], preserves this locality for depth, but its corrections are not expressed as 3D point differences, making them poorly aligned with the point map losses. We therefore extend the pairwise comparison to point maps by matching depth-normalized 3D finite differences: (cid:101)xQij = xQij min([Qij]z, [Qi,j+1]z) , (2) where is point map, [Qij]z denotes the coordinate of Qij, and (cid:101)y is defined analogously for vertical pairs. We normalize by since our decoder represents predicted points as (ξeρ, ηeρ, eρ), where = eρ is the scalar scale factor shared by all three coordinates. For neighboring pair, we use the nearer endpoint depth as conservative local scale. Our point gradient matching loss averages Euclidean distances between corresponding predicted and ground-truth normalized finite differences over the same valid-pair sets: Lpgm( ˆP, P) = 1 2Vx (cid:88) (i,j)Vx (cid:13) (cid:13) (cid:101)x ˆPij (cid:101)xPij (cid:13) (cid:13) (cid:13) (cid:13)2 + 1 2Vy (cid:88) (i,j)Vy (cid:13) (cid:13) (cid:101)y ˆPij (cid:101)yPij (cid:13) (cid:13) (cid:13) (cid:13)2 . (3) Python-like version is given in Pseudocode A. Our proposed Lpgm combines the following useful properties: the residual is computed on full 3D displacements, so it supervises both local direction and magnitude, while the depth normalization makes the comparison scale-invariant for each neighboring pair. In practice, we evaluate Lpgm only on pairs whose two endpoints are annotated and omit pairs near occlusion boundaries. At such boundaries, adjacent pixels often belong to different surfaces and the exact ground-truth transition depends on dataset-specific edge conventions and resampling. Masking these pairs removes largely irreducible residuals and slightly improves training. We choose the relative weight of Lpgm empirically, finding that coefficient of 10 gives good balance with the global point loss. Our full dense-label objective is: = Lglob + Lloc,4 + Lloc,16 + Lloc,64 + 10Lpgm. For noisier or sparse annotations, we omit high-frequency terms according to label quality; see Sec. C.

sections.title: 4 Experiments
sections.content: 4.1 Experimental Setup Training. Unless otherwise stated, we use the DINOv2 [29]-Large encoder and NAD described in Sec. 3.1. We train on balanced mix of twenty synthetic and real datasets covering outdoor, indoor, in-the-wild, driving, and object-centric domains [2, 7, 11, 13, 18, 19, 22, 23, 27, 32, 35, 41, 42, 48, 49, 53, 57, 62, 64, 67, 69]. The dataset weights are listed in Sec. D. Following common practice [3, 50], we adapt supervision to label quality: for synthetic labels we use the full objective in Sec. 3, for SfM labels we omit Lloc,64 and Lpgm, and for LiDAR labels we keep only Lglob and Lloc,4. We optimize with AdamW [26] for 120K steps at total batch size 128, using peak learning rates 3 104 for the decoder and 3 105 for the backbone, and reciprocal square root schedule [65] with 1K warmup steps and 10% cooldown. The first 80% of training uses low-resolution budget of 1024 encoder tokens with fixed image area and sampled target aspect ratios. The final 20% uses higher resolutions with encoder-token budgets between 1024 and 2802, and the final 10% samples only synthetic data. More details are given in Sec. C. Every ablation follows the final models full training protocol and changes only the component under study. This full-scale protocol is deliberate: several effects observed in smaller proxy runs did not persist at the final scale. full run takes roughly 31 hours on 16 H100 GPUs for the low-resolution phase and another 8 hours on 32 H100 GPUs for the high-resolution phase. Evaluation. We evaluate zero-shot on eight common monocular geometry benchmarks: NYUv2 [39], KITTI [44], ETH3D [37], iBims-1 [21], GSO [10], Sintel [4], DDAD [12], and DIODE [45]. Following MoGe [50], we report ROE-aligned affine-invariant point map AbsRel metrics in global and instance-wise local forms. Let be the set of all valid pixels and let ˆP ij = ˆPij + denote aligned predictions, where is shared scale factor and is 3D translation. The global absolute relative error is defined as AbsRelglob = 1 (cid:88) (i,j)V (cid:13) ˆP (cid:13) (cid:13) ij Pij Pij2 (cid:13) (cid:13) (cid:13)2 . For AbsRelloc, we evaluate each instance with segmentation region Ωr over Vr = Ωr. We estimate separate local scale-and-translation alignment ˆP,r ij = sr ˆPij + tr, and normalize residuals (cid:13) by the ground-truth instance diameter dr = (cid:13) (cid:13), where max and min are taken component-wise: (cid:13)max(i,j)Vr Pij min(i,j)Vr Pij AbsRelloc = 1 (cid:88) rR 1 Vr (cid:88) (i,j)Vr (cid:13) ˆP,r (cid:13) (cid:13) ij Pij dr (cid:13) (cid:13) (cid:13)2 . For our proposed point map normal mean angular error (MAEnormal), we first form four local normals around each pixel from cross products of adjacent point map differences. The valid local normals are averaged, giving normal maps Nij and ˆNij for the ground truth and prediction. We report MAEnormal = 1 VN (cid:88) ( ˆNij, Nij) (i,j)VN (4) in degrees, where VN contains pixels whose annotated neighborhood defines at least one valid local normal. We report AbsRelloc on benchmarks with instance masks, and MAEnormal where dense ground truth supports reliable normal estimation. Lower is better for all metrics. 4.2 Comparison to the State of the Art We compare SurGe against recent feedforward 3D reconstruction methods. The clearest gains appear in the local evaluations, AbsRelloc and MAEnormal. These evaluations are complementary: AbsRelloc measures pointwise point map accuracy after instance-level alignment, whereas MAEnormal evaluates the local surface orientation induced by neighboring point predictions. In Tab. 1, SurGe gives the lowest AbsRelloc on every evaluated dataset. The same pattern holds for MAEnormal in Tab. 2, 7 Table 1: SotA comparison for AbsRelloc (%). uses MoGe-2 for alignment and unprojection. Table 3 shows that global point map accuracy is already highly competitive among the strongest recent models, especially MoGe, MoGe-2 [51], and π3 [54]. Within this tighter regime, SurGe remains consistently strong: it ranks first on four of the eight datasets and obtains the best average rank overall. Thus, gains in the local evaluations do not come at the expense of global scene geometry. The qualitative examples in Fig. 1 show the same trend. MoGe-2 and InfiniDepth [63] recover plausible coarse shape but produce irregular local surfaces, visible in both the rendered 3D geometry and the point map normals. In contrast, SurGe preserves thin structures and produces smoother surfaces with sharper geometric detail. Together, the quantitative and qualitative results show that SurGe improves local surface quality while preserving state-of-the-art global point map accuracy. 4.3 Ablation Study Decoder design. We compare SurGes Neighborhood Attention Decoder (NAD) against DPT head following VGGT [47], MoGes ConvStack [50], larger ConvStack-L baseline that matches our stage layout, widths, and depth while keeping convolutional residual blocks, and ViT [9] decoder following π3 [54]. To isolate the effect of the decoder architecture, we attach each decoder to the same DINOv2 ViT-Large backbone and use the same point map output parameterization and loss, including Lpgm. Tables 4 to 6 show that increasing convolutional decoder capacity helps: ConvStack-L moderately improves over ConvStack on most datasets in both local and global evaluations. However, our NAD performs even better, achieving the lowest error on every dataset in the AbsRelloc and MAEnormal evaluations, and on seven of eight datasets for global point map accuracy. The ViT decoder is competitive on some datasets for the global evaluation, but has the highest error on every dataset in the local evaluations; patch-level artifacts are one visible failure mode. 8 Table 4: Decoder ablation for AbsRelloc (%). Compared to SurGe, the model with the ConvStack-L decoder slightly warps thin structures such as the chair legs and misplaces parts of the wall on the right side. Surface loss. We next ablate the surface loss while keeping the NAD and the pointwise global and local losses fixed. We compare supervision from point map normals [50] (Lnormal), log-depth gradient matching on the coordinate [20, 23] (Lgm), and our point gradient matching loss (Lpgm). As shown in Tabs. 7 and 8, both gradient-based losses improve the local evaluations compared to Lnormal, and our proposed Lpgm improves over Lgm in most cases. However, in the global evaluation shown in Tab. 9, Lgm generally performs worse than Lnormal, while Lpgm noticeably outperforms both, which we attribute to the fact that the Lpgm signal remains in the same space as the global loss (i.e., 3D point displacements). Overall, Lpgm provides practical replacement for prior surface losses, improving local surface quality without sacrificing global point map accuracy.

sections.title: 5 Conclusion
sections.content: We presented SurGe, monocular point map model designed to improve local surface geometry rather than only average point accuracy. SurGe improves the local point map and point map normal performance across eight zero-shot benchmarks while preserving strong global geometry; ablations show that both the NAD head and Lpgm contribute to these gains. Beyond the model, our point map normal metric is step toward improved geometric evaluation: it exposes surface artifacts that are only weakly captured by pointwise metrics. We hope that this will make local surface quality more explicit target for future geometry models. Our ablations further suggest that current decoder designs are limiting factor in predicting high-quality local surface geometry. Since the NAD head increases computational cost relative to smaller convolutional decoders, more efficient variants are useful direction for future work. Acknowledgements. This work has received funding from the Jupiter AI Factory (JAIF), jointly funded by the European High Performance Computing Joint Undertaking (JU), the German Federal Ministry of Research, Technology and Space (BMFTR), and the Ministry of Culture and Science of North Rhine-Westphalia (MKW NRW) under grant agreement No. 101250682. The authors gratefully acknowledge the Gauss Centre for Supercomputing e.V. (www.gauss-centre.eu) for providing computing time through the John von Neumann Institute for Computing (NIC) on the GCS Supercomputers JUWELS and JUPITER at Jülich Supercomputing Centre (JSC).

sections.title: A Details on the Point Gradient Matching Loss
sections.content: Pseudocode gives Python-like specification of Lpgm in Eq. (3). The loss optimizes the orientation and magnitude of local point gradients by matching 3D displacements; normalization by makes the loss scale-invariant. We compute Lpgm only on point pairs where all involved points are valid. Pseudocode A: Point gradient matching loss Lpgm def point_gradient_loss(pred, gt, valid): # pred, gt: [B, H, W, 3] # valid: [B, H, W] def pair_loss(pred0, pred1, gt0, gt1, valid0, valid1): valid_pair = valid0 & valid pred_grad = (pred1 - pred0) / minimum(pred0[..., 2:], pred1[..., 2:]) gt_grad = (gt1 - gt0) / minimum(gt0[..., 2:], gt1[..., 2:]) loss = (pred_grad - gt_grad).norm(dim=-1) return (loss * valid_pair).sum() / valid_pair.sum() loss_dx = pair_loss( pred[:, :, :-1, :], pred[:, :, 1:, :], gt[:, :, :-1, :], valid[:, :, :-1], gt[:, :, 1:, :], valid[:, :, 1:], ) loss_dy = pair_loss( pred[:, :-1, :, :], pred[:, 1:, :, :], gt[:, :-1, :, :], valid[:, :-1, :], gt[:, 1:, :, :], valid[:, 1:, :], ) return (loss_dx + loss_dy) /

sections.title: B Architectural Details
sections.content: Window-matched RoPE. In addition to the embedded UV coordinates added at the beginning of each decoder stage, we apply RoPE [40] to the queries and keys in each NAD block. The UV grid provides absolute coordinates, while RoPE gives each attention head consistent representation of relative offsets inside local windows, making local attention patterns easier to reuse across neighborhoods. We adapt the RoPE base frequency to the kernel size where attention is computed. For Neighborhood Attention window size k, we set the base frequency τ = k/π. For head dimension dh, axial 2D RoPE uses = dh/4 geometrically spaced frequency bands per spatial axis, with frequencies ωm = τ m/M for = 0, . . . , 1. With this choice, the lowest-frequency band changes by π radians, i.e., half rotation, over one neighborhood width, providing meaningful phase variation within each local window. In preliminary experiments, RoPE with this window-matched base frequency improves accuracy over using only the UV embedding. Normalization in NAD blocks. We remove the usual normalization layers before the attention and FFN blocks. Wang et al. [51] remove normalization layers from the ConvStack without observing instabilities or reduced accuracy. In our attention decoder, removing LayerNorm [1] improves accuracy, but occasionally leads to irrecoverable training instabilities. We tried to replace LayerNorm with several alternative normalization approaches (RMSNorm [66], DyT [68], and LayerScale [43]), and found that each of them recovers training stability, but none of them improves accuracy over LayerNorm. We therefore suspect that per-token normalization can interfere with the unbounded regression task of 3D geometry estimation. To stabilize training without compromising task performance, we keep the norm-free block structure and apply QK normalization [6, 65] in the NAD blocks. 14 Figure A: Learning rate schedule. We use reciprocal square root learning rate schedule [65] (rsqrt) with timescale of 2400, linear warmup of 1000 steps, and 10% cooldown phase with schedule 1 + lrmin, where (0, 1] is the progress of the cooldown phase and lrmin is the peak learning rate multiplied by 102. Early training is stable enough to support much larger peak learning rate than we can use with standard cosine decay. The rsqrt schedule uses this large initial rate to make rapid progress, then quickly decays to moderate rate, whereas cosine schedule at the same peak becomes unstable later in training. In our setting, rsqrt tends to outperform cosine decay even when the total number of training steps is known in advance, while still allowing training to continue indefinitely before cooldown. Stage-wise UV embedding. Following Wang et al. [50], we add learned positional embedding to the features at the beginning of each stage in the NAD head. This embedding is produced by linearly projecting UV grid at the stage resolution, with coordinates normalized by the image diagonal. It gives the decoder aspect-ratio-aware absolute position information for the current feature map.

sections.title: C Training Details
sections.content: All main models and ablations are trained for 120 000 optimizer steps with total batch size 128. We use AdamW [26] with peak learning rate 3 104 for the decoder and 3 105 for the DINOv2 [29] backbone, gradient clipping at 1.0, and the reciprocal square root schedule shown in Fig. A. The decoder learning rate is linearly warmed up for 1000 steps, during which the backbone remains frozen. After unfreezing, the backbone learning rate is also warmed up for 1000 steps, ending at 0.1 times the decoder schedule evaluated at step 2000; after this point, it remains phase-aligned with the decoder schedule at the same 0.1 scale. Training uses bf16 mixed precision for the backbone, while the point decoder, output remapping, and losses are evaluated in fp32. For the first 80% of training, we sample images at fixed area of 5122 pixels with target aspect ratios in [0.5, 2.0], and use low-resolution encoder-token budget of 1024. The final 20% of steps sample image areas uniformly from [5122, 9602] pixels and encoder-token budgets uniformly from {1024, . . . , 2802}. The cooldown phase in the final 10% samples only high-quality synthetic data: all synthetic training datasets except Ken Burns [27] and G-Objaverse [7, 32]. Following common practice [3, 50], we adapt supervision to label quality by selecting loss terms by label type: Lsynthetic = Lglob + Lloc,4 + Lloc,16 + Lloc,64 + 10Lpgm, Lsfm = Lglob + Lloc,4 + Lloc,16, Llidar = Lglob + Lloc,4.

sections.title: D Training Data
sections.content: We train on subset of the datasets used by MoGe-2 [51]; the full list is provided in Tab. A. All datasets used are publicly available for academic use. 15 Table A: List of training datasets. The mix and weighting are similar to MoGe-2 [51].

sections.content: 7.4 8.6 12.0 4.8 2.8 5.0 5.6 1.6 1.3 5.6 4.0 1.2 4.8 1.1 0.1 5.0 14.1 1.6 2.1 6.4 Table B: Runtime comparison with ConvStack-L. Inference latency is reported on an NVIDIA H100 GPU as the median over 50 timed iterations at 512512 resolution and batch size 1, after 10 warmup iterations and with CUDA synchronization. All benchmarks use torch.compile with dynamic shapes. The encoders are run with bf16 autocast, while decoders are evaluated in fp32 to preserve output quality. Peak memory is measured with PyTorch max_memory_allocated. Encoder DINOv2-giant DINOv2-giant DINOv2-Large DINOv2-Large None None Decoder ConvStack-L NAD (ours) ConvStack-L NAD (ours) ConvStack-L NAD (ours)

sections.title: E Decoder Runtime Tradeoff
sections.content: Inference latency (ms) Relative Peak memory (GiB) 20.89 27.15 14.53 21.15 5.94 13. 1.00 1.30 1.00 1.46 1.00 2.28 5.02 5.23 1.87 2.02 0.72 0. NAD improves local surface quality over the convolutional decoder baselines at the cost of higher inference latency. Table quantifies this runtime tradeoff at 512512 resolution. The decoder alone is 2.28 slower than ConvStack-L. In the full model, the slowdown is smaller: 1.30 with DINOv2-giant and 1.46 with DINOv2-Large, since the shared encoder accounts for part of total inference time. Peak memory increases modestly with NAD. Thus, NAD is an accuracy-oriented decoder design, and improving the efficiency of Neighborhood Attention decoding is an important direction for future work.

sections.title: F Additional Qualitative Examples
sections.content: Figures and compare SurGe with recent methods on in-the-wild scenes with large depth ranges. PPD [58] and InfiniDepth [63] struggle most in this setting: both recover plausible near-field geometry but strongly compress distant scene structure. Their normal maps further show strong surface noise for PPD and visible surface artifacts for InfiniDepth. VGGT [47] is less affected but still produces broken near-field geometry or slightly compressed distant structure. MoGe [50] and MoGe-2 [51] are closest to SurGe in global layout, but their rendered point maps and normals show less coherent local surface geometry, including bending and oscillatory artifacts. Figures to show additional in-the-wild predictions from SurGe. 17 Predicted Point Map Near View ] 7 4 [ V ] 3 6 [ e fi ] 8 5 [ ] 1 5 [ 2 - M ] 0 5 [ M u Figure B: Qualitative comparison of state-of-the-art methods in high-dynamic-range scene. The top-left panel shows the input image. Each row compares one method; columns show point map normals, followed by birds-eye and near-camera renderings of the predicted point map. VGGT, InfiniDepth, and PPD struggle with the high dynamic range and collapse distant geometry. VGGT oversmooths surfaces, InfiniDepth, MoGe-2, and MoGe introduce oscillatory or bending artifacts on the table and bench legs, and PPD produces heavy surface noise. SurGe exhibits the best local surface geometry with sharp edges and straight surfaces. Best viewed zoomed in. 18 Predicted Point Map Near View ] 7 4 [ V ] 3 6 [ e fi ] 8 5 [ P ] 1 5 [ 2 - M ] 0 5 [ M u Figure C: Qualitative comparison of state-of-the-art methods in high-dynamic-range scene. The top-left panel shows the input image. Each row compares one method; columns show point map normals, followed by birds-eye and near-camera renderings of the predicted point map. InfiniDepth and PPD struggle with the high dynamic range, VGGT fails to reconstruct geometry close to the camera, and SurGe produces cleaner local surface geometry than MoGe. Best viewed zoomed in. 19 Figure D: Additional qualitative results from SurGe. Each example includes the RGB input, predicted depth, point map normals, and rendered point map. 20 Figure E: Additional qualitative results from SurGe. Each example includes the RGB input, predicted depth, point map normals, and rendered point map. 21 Figure F: Additional qualitative results from SurGe. Each example includes the RGB input, predicted depth, point map normals, and rendered point map.
